en.extract:
The golden-tailed woodpecker (Campethera abingoni) is a species of bird in the family Picidae. Its specific name commemorates the 5th Earl of Abingdon. It belongs to a species complex that includes the Knysna woodpecker to the south of its range, and the mostly allopatric Mombasa woodpecker to the northeast, with which it perhaps hybridizes.


Description
The combination of barred, greenish upper part plumage, and streaked underparts is distinctive. Their golden-olive tails do not differ markedly from those of several related or sympatric woodpecker species, but their single, strident call-note is characteristic.
It measures 20 to 21 cm from bill tip to tail tip. The southern races weigh about 70 g, but the northern race C. a. chrysura, only about 55 g. Males are on average larger and heavier than females. The sexes are best distinguished by their head markings, as the red and brown fore-crowns of males differ unmistakably from those of females that are black and spotted white. The malar stripes of males are red and that of females mottled black and white. The iris is usually dark red but variable, the mandibles slate grey, and the legs and feet greenish-olive. Juveniles are heavily streaked on the throat and breast and barred on the belly. They and have mottled malar stripes, and a brown to brownish-grey iris.


Sound communication
Besides the single strident "waaa" or "weeea" call-note, usually by the male, it has a long-range and repeated yaooaak-yaaaaaak. It drums softly.


Habitat

It is present in coastal forest, miombo, mopane and acacia woodlands. It shows a preference for riparian woodland, particularly in the dryer parts of southern Africa. It may intensively forage small areas in the lower to middle strata of trees. It establishes fairly large territories of 10 to 15 ha, and can cover some distance between foraging sites.


Range
Very widespread in woodlands or forest south of the equator, and very localized north of it. It is found in Angola, Benin, Botswana, Cameroon, Central African Republic, Chad, Republic of the Congo, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Eswatini, Ivory Coast, Gambia, Ghana, Guinea, Guinea-Bissau, Kenya, Malawi, Mali, Mauritania, Mozambique, Namibia, Rwanda, Senegal, South Africa, South Sudan, Tanzania, Uganda, Zambia, and Zimbabwe.


Feeding
It obtains most food by probing and gleaning, but also hammers dead or infected wood rather more frequently than other Campethera species. It feeds mostly on arboreal ants and termites, but also takes millipedes, wood-boring larvae and leaf-feeding moth larvae. It occurs alongside the Cardinal woodpecker which to some extent exploits thinner branches.


Nesting
They nest during early summer. Both sexes excavate the nest, usually on the underside of a branch in the lower to middle level of a tree. Two to three glossy white eggs are laid, and the parents take turns to incubate them. The male incubates at night. The incubation period is about 13 days, and the chicks are fed regurgitated food by both parents. Chicks leave the nest after 22 to 25 days. Their nests are parasitized by various species of honeyguide.


Races
There are 6 to 7 accepted races. C. a. chrysura (Swainson, 1837)  occurs from Senegambia to western Uganda. It is quite green above, has streaked ear coverts and is smaller in size than either C. a. suahelica or C. a. abingoni, without overlap. The tropical race C. a. kavirondensis van Someren, 1926 is olive-green above and more broadly barred, with thinner streaking below. C. a. suahelica (Reichenow, 1902) which occurs from the Kilimanjaro region to northern Eswatini, is similar to the former but has a yellower toned upper parts. The nominate race is widespread in arid and mesic woodlands of southern Africa, and has denser throat streaking than C. a. suahelica. C. a. anderssoni (Roberts, 1936) occurs from southwestern Angola to northwestern South Africa. It has very dense throat and breast streaking, locally verging on solid black. C. a. constricta Clancey, 1965 which occurs from southern Mozambique to KwaZulu-Natal, is smaller than the nominate race, and has the upper part plumage greener and under part plumage more yellowish.


Similar species
The Mombasa woodpecker has yellower plumage below, has brighter golden-green upper parts, with small white dots and no barring (and never drums). Cardinal, Bennett's, Reichenow's and Nubian woodpeckers are all browner above and more clearly barred, and only the first of these is streaked below.


Gallery


References


External links
Golden-tailed woodpecker - Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds

The '''golden-tailed woodpecker''' ('''''Campethera abingoni''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Picidae]]. Its specific name commemorates the [[Montagu Bertie, 5th Earl of Abingdon|5th Earl of Abingdon]].<ref name="beo">{{cite book |last1=Beolens |first1=Bo |last2=Watkins |first2=Michael |last3=Grayson |first3=Michael |title=The Eponym Dictionary of Birds|date=2014|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |isbn=978-1-4729-0574-1}}</ref> It belongs to a [[species complex]] that includes the [[Knysna woodpecker]] to the south of its range, and the mostly allopatric<ref name=zim/> [[Mombasa woodpecker]] to the northeast,<ref name="ibc">{{cite web|last1=Winkler |first1=H.|last2=Christie|first2=D. A. |last3=Kirwan |first3=G. M. |title=Golden-tailed Woodpecker (Campethera abingoni) | date = 2016  |url=http://www.hbw.com/species/golden-tailed-woodpecker-campethera-abingoni#Taxonomy |website=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive |publisher=Lynx Edicions, Barcelona|access-date=25 July 2016}}</ref> with which it perhaps hybridizes.

==Description==
The combination of barred, greenish upper part plumage, and streaked underparts is distinctive. Their golden-olive tails do not differ markedly from those of several related or sympatric woodpecker species, but their single, strident call-note is characteristic.

It [[Bird measurement|measures]] 20<ref name=zim>{{cite book |last=Zimmerman |first=Dale A. |title=Birds of Kenya and Northern Tanzania |year=1999 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=0-691-01022-6 |page=491 |display-authors=etal}}</ref> to 21 cm<ref name=geo/> from bill tip to tail tip.<ref name=geo/> The southern races weigh about 70 g,<ref name=geo/> but the northern race ''C. a. chrysura'', only about 55 g. Males are on average larger and heavier than females.<ref name="kei"/> The sexes are best distinguished by their head markings, as the red and brown fore-crowns of males differ unmistakably from those of females that are black and spotted white. The malar stripes of males are red and that of females mottled black and white. The iris is usually dark red but variable,<ref name="wink"/> the mandibles slate grey,<ref name=zim/> and the legs and feet greenish-olive.<ref name="kei">{{cite book|last1=Keith|first1=Stuart|last2=Urban|first2=Emil K. |last3=Fry |first3=C. Hilary |title=The Birds of Africa, Volume III|date=1992|publisher=Academic Press |isbn=978-0-12-137303-0 |pages=525–526}}</ref> Juveniles are heavily streaked on the throat and breast and barred on the belly. They and have mottled malar stripes,<ref name=zim/> and a brown to brownish-grey iris.<ref name="wink"/>

==Sound communication==
Besides the single strident "waaa"<ref name=ginn>{{cite book |last1=Ginn |first1=Peter |title=Birds of the Highveld |date=1981 |publisher=Longman |location=Salisbury |isbn=0-582-60890-2 |pages=[https://archive.org/details/birdsofhighveld0000ginn/page/93 93–94] |edition=3rd impr. |url=https://archive.org/details/birdsofhighveld0000ginn/page/93 }}</ref> or "weeea"<ref name="wink">{{cite book |last1=Winkler |first1=Hans |last2=Christie|first2=David A. |last3=Nurney |first3=David |title=Woodpeckers |date=2010 |publisher=A & C Black |location=London |isbn=978-1-4081-3503-7 |pages=72, 230–231}}</ref> call-note, usually by the male, it has a long-range and repeated ''yaooaak-yaaaaaak''.<ref name="wink"/> It drums softly.<ref name=zim/>

==Habitat==
[[File:Morning Clean Up - Golden Tailed Woodpecker.jpg|thumb|right|Female preening after daybreak]]
It is present in coastal forest, [[miombo]], [[mopane]] and [[Acacia sensu lato|acacia]] woodlands. It shows a preference for riparian woodland, particularly in the dryer parts of southern Africa.<ref name=ginn/> It may intensively forage small areas in the lower to middle strata of trees.<ref name=zim/> It establishes fairly large territories of 10 to 15 [[hectare|ha]], and can cover some distance between foraging sites.<ref name="wink"/>

==Range==
Very widespread in woodlands or forest south of the equator, and very localized north of it. It is found in [[Angola]], [[Benin]], [[Botswana]], [[Cameroon]], [[Central African Republic]], [[Chad]], [[Republic of the Congo]], [[Democratic Republic of the Congo]], [[Eswatini]], [[Ivory Coast]], [[Gambia]], [[Ghana]], [[Guinea]], [[Guinea-Bissau]], [[Kenya]], [[Malawi]], [[Mali]], [[Mauritania]], [[Mozambique]], [[Namibia]], [[Rwanda]], [[Senegal]], [[South Africa]], [[South Sudan]], [[Tanzania]], [[Uganda]], [[Zambia]], and [[Zimbabwe]].

==Feeding==
It obtains most food by probing and gleaning, but also hammers dead or infected wood rather more frequently than other ''Campethera'' species.<ref name="wink"/> It feeds mostly on arboreal ants and termites, but also takes millipedes, wood-boring larvae and leaf-feeding moth larvae. It occurs alongside the Cardinal woodpecker which to some extent exploits thinner branches.<ref name="wink"/>

==Nesting==
They nest during early summer. Both sexes excavate the nest, usually on the underside of a branch in the lower to middle level of a tree. Two to three glossy white eggs are laid, and the parents take turns to incubate them. The male incubates at night.<ref name="wink"/> The incubation period is about 13 days, and the chicks are fed regurgitated food by both parents. Chicks leave the nest after 22 to 25 days. Their nests are parasitized by various species of [[Indicator (genus)|honeyguide]].<ref name="biodiv">{{cite web|title=''Campethera abingoni'' (Golden-tailed woodpecker) |url=http://www.biodiversityexplorer.org/birds/picidae/campethera_abingoni.htm |website=biodiversityexplorer.org |publisher=Iziko Museums |access-date=25 July 2016}}</ref>

==Races==
There are 6<ref name="ibc"/> to 7<ref name=geo>{{cite book |last=Chittenden |first=H. |title=Roberts geographic variation of southern African birds |year=2012 |publisher=JVBBF |location=Cape Town |isbn=978-1-920602-00-0 |pages=100–101 |display-authors=etal}}</ref> accepted races. ''C. a. chrysura'' <small>(Swainson, 1837)</small>  occurs from Senegambia to western Uganda. It is quite green above, has streaked ear coverts and is smaller in size than either ''C. a. suahelica'' or ''C. a. abingoni'', without overlap. The tropical race ''C. a. kavirondensis'' <small>van Someren, 1926</small> is olive-green above and more broadly barred, with thinner streaking below. ''C. a. suahelica'' <small>(Reichenow, 1902)</small> which occurs from the Kilimanjaro region to northern Eswatini, is similar to the former but has a yellower toned upper parts. The nominate race is widespread in arid and mesic woodlands of southern Africa, and has denser throat streaking than ''C. a. suahelica''. ''C. a. anderssoni'' <small>(Roberts, 1936)</small> occurs from southwestern Angola to northwestern South Africa. It has very dense throat and breast streaking, locally verging on solid black. ''C. a. constricta'' <small>Clancey, 1965</small> which occurs from southern Mozambique to KwaZulu-Natal, is smaller than the nominate race, and has the upper part plumage greener and under part plumage more yellowish.<ref name=geo/>

==Similar species==
The Mombasa woodpecker has yellower plumage below,<ref name=zim/> has brighter golden-green upper parts, with small white dots and no barring (and never drums). [[Cardinal woodpecker|Cardinal]], [[Bennett's woodpecker|Bennett's]], [[Reichenow's woodpecker|Reichenow's]] and [[Nubian woodpecker]]s are all browner above and more clearly barred,<ref name="wink"/> and only the first of these is streaked below.
